title: BUPT Operating Systems Course Labs (buptLab-os)
summary: A six-lab operating systems course suite for BUPT's 2024-2025 semester, covering process management, POSIX thread synchronization, inter-process communication, synchronization/mutex theory, multi-threaded parallel computation optimization, and process address-space observation. Each lab is an independent C++ assignment tested by building the binary and inspecting stdout.
instructionPreview: This is an undergraduate operating-systems lab suite covering process management, inter-process communication, POSIX thread synchronization, and multithreading optimization. You must implement all the modules and features listed below so that all tests pass.
instruction:
This is an undergraduate operating-systems lab suite covering process management,
inter-process communication, POSIX thread synchronization, and multithreading optimization.
You must implement all the modules and features listed below so that all tests pass.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out:

- **lab2-thread** (`lab2-thread/`): POSIX thread synchronization simulations. Task 1
  (`task1/src/main.cpp`) and the matching Lab 4 variant (`lab4-synmut/problem3-hospital/posix/src/main.cpp`)
  implement a hospital queue where patient and doctor threads coordinate via mutexes,
  semaphores, and condition variables. Task 2 (`task2/src/main.cpp`) implements a dormitory
  room simulation where students decide whether to study based on mutex-protected shared
  state and probability checks. (~3 files, ~4 functions to implement)

- **lab3-ipc** (`lab3-ipc/`): Four inter-process communication mechanisms, each in its
  own subdirectory. Each must implement writer and reader functions plus a main function
  that sets up the IPC channel, forks child processes, runs writer/reader, and cleans up
  resources. The four mechanisms are: System V message queues (`task1-message/`), shared
  memory with semaphore synchronization (`task2-shared_memory/`), unnamed pipes
  (`task3-pipe/subtask1-unnamed/`), and named pipes/FIFOs (`task3-pipe/subtask2-named/`).
  A signal-handling demonstration (`task4-signal/`) implements signal handlers for parent
  and child processes using fork. (~5 files, ~18 functions to implement)

- **lab5-multithreading** (`lab5-multithreading/`): A computation benchmark exercising
  different parallelization strategies. The shared header `src/config.hpp` defines bitwise
  arithmetic operations (add, sub, mul, div) that operate on uint32_t values using only
  bit-level manipulation. Seven source files implement variants of the same computation
  with different threading strategies: serial execution, parallel-2 (two threads),
  parallel-3 (three threads), with CPU affinity, with mutex synchronization, with cache-line
  padding, and combined mutex+padding. Each file has `part_a`, `part_b`, `solve` functions
  in the `apple_solution` namespace, a `solve` function in the `orange_solution` namespace,
  and a `main` function. (~8 files, ~32 functions to implement)

- **lab1-process/task2** (`lab1-process/task2/solution.cpp`): Three multiplication
  implementations. `solution_a` computes the product directly, `solution_b` uses
  repeated addition, and `solution_c` uses nested counting loops. Each writes its result
  to `task->answer`. (~1 file, ~3 functions to implement)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called directly by the test suite (via compiled binaries).
You must implement them with these exact signatures:

- `patient(void *arg) -> void *` in `lab2-thread/task1/src/main.cpp` and
  `lab4-synmut/problem3-hospital/posix/src/main.cpp` -- coordinate patient thread
  lifecycle: ticket acquisition, queuing, waiting for treatment, and departure
- `doctor(void *arg) -> void *` in `lab2-thread/task1/src/main.cpp` and
  `lab4-synmut/problem3-hospital/posix/src/main.cpp` -- coordinate doctor thread
  lifecycle: waiting for patients, dequeuing, treating, with cleanup handler
- `DormRoom::study(size_t id, const std::chrono::steady_clock::time_point last_study) -> void`
  in `lab2-thread/task2/src/main.cpp` -- manage study session with mutex-protected
  active count and timing messages
- `DormRoom::check_and_decide(const size_t id, std::mt19937 &gen, std::uniform_real_distribution<> dis) -> bool`
  in `lab2-thread/task2/src/main.cpp` -- probability-based decision to join studying

Build each lab with `make build` in its directory. For lab5-multithreading, use `bash build.sh`
in the lab5-multithreading directory.

moduleDag.description: A six-lab operating systems course suite for BUPT's 2024-2025 semester, covering process management, POSIX thread synchronization, inter-process communication, synchronization/mutex theory, multi-threaded parallel computation optimization, and process address-space observation. Each lab is an independent C++ assignment tested by building the binary and inspecting stdout.

nodes.label: Lab1 Process Task2 — Multiplication Implementations
nodes.description: Three C++ functions (solution_a, solution_b, solution_c) that compute the product of two uint32_t values using direct multiplication, repeated addition, and nested counting loops respectively, each writing the 64-bit result into task->answer.

nodes.label: Lab2 Thread Task1 — Hospital Queue (Patient/Doctor Threads)
nodes.description: Concurrent hospital simulation where patient threads acquire numbered tickets via mutex, enqueue themselves, and wait on per-patient condition variables for treatment; doctor threads loop on a semaphore, dequeue patients, signal treatment start, and use pthread_cleanup_push for cancellation safety.

nodes.label: Lab2 Thread Task2 — Dormitory Study Simulation
nodes.description: DormRoom class where student threads decide probabilistically whether to join an ongoing study session; study() increments/decrements a mutex-protected active_count and times the session, while check_and_decide() applies two-stage probability checks against the shared count.

nodes.label: Lab3 IPC Task1 — System V Message Queue
nodes.description: Writer/reader throughput benchmark via System V message queue: parent writes N frames with msgsnd, forked child reads them with msgrcv; both report per-interval and total MiB/s; parent destroys the queue with msgctl.

nodes.label: Lab3 IPC Task2 — Shared Memory with Semaphore Synchronization
nodes.description: Writer/reader throughput benchmark via System V shared memory protected by a two-semaphore producer-consumer lock pair (sem_p/sem_v on write_semid and read_semid); parent creates shared memory and semaphores, forks reader, then cleans up all IPC resources.

nodes.label: Lab3 IPC Task3a — Unnamed Pipe
nodes.description: Writer/reader throughput benchmark via anonymous pipe; writer sends frames in a partial-write loop, reader reassembles frames with a partial-read loop, and main() forks and assigns read/write ends of pipe(2) to parent and child respectively.

nodes.label: Lab3 IPC Task3b — Named Pipe (FIFO)
nodes.description: Writer/reader throughput benchmark via FIFO created with mkfifo; parent opens for writing and acts as writer, forked child opens for reading; parent waits for child and unlinks the FIFO after all frames are transferred.

nodes.label: Lab3 IPC Task4 — Signal Handling
nodes.description: Parent forks N children each waiting for a distinct signal with a local handler that prints PID and exits; parent installs a global_handler for SIGTSTP that forwards the appropriate signal to each child and waits for all children to exit.

nodes.label: Lab4 Sync/Mutex Problem3 — Hospital Queue (POSIX)
nodes.description: Formal synchronization exercise re-using the hospital queue pattern from lab2-thread/task1: same patient/doctor logic with ticket mutex, per-patient condition variables, semaphore, and cleanup handlers — studied here as the canonical POSIX sync/mutex solution to a producer-consumer problem.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Bitwise Arithmetic Operations (config.hpp)
nodes.description: Shared header defining bitwise-only implementations of add (ripple- carry adder), sub (two's complement), mul (shift-and-add), and div (binary long division) on uint32_t; these four lambdas in the operations namespace are included and called by every lab5 computation file.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Serial Baseline
nodes.description: Establishes the dual-problem computation pattern: apple_solution runs part_a and part_b sequentially (joins each before creating the next), and main() also runs apple_thread and orange_thread sequentially, serving as the reference baseline for all parallel variants.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-2 (Apple/Orange Concurrent)
nodes.description: First parallel variant: main() launches apple_thread and orange_thread concurrently (creates both before joining either) to measure task-level parallelism; apple_solution still runs part_a and part_b sequentially inside the apple thread.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-2 with CPU Affinity
nodes.description: Extends parallel_2 by calling set_cpu({0,1}) inside apple_solution::solve and set_cpu({2,3}) inside orange_solution::solve, pinning each top-level task thread to a dedicated CPU pair.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-3 (Full 3-Thread Parallel)
nodes.description: Extends parallel_2 by making apple_solution::solve create thread_a and thread_b concurrently (creates both before joining either), so that all three logical threads (apple-a, apple-b, orange) run simultaneously.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-3 with CPU Affinity
nodes.description: Adds per-thread CPU affinity to parallel_3: part_a binds to {0,1}, part_b binds to {2,3}, and orange_solution::solve binds to {4,5}, pinning each of the three concurrent threads to a dedicated CPU set.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-3 with Mutex
nodes.description: Adds a pthread_mutex_t guard around add_and_assign in part_a and part_b to serialise writes to shared apple_t fields, demonstrating the cost of contention when eliminating the data race introduced by parallel_3.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-3 with Cache-Line Padding
nodes.description: Inserts cache-line padding in apple_t so that apple->a and apple->b reside on separate cache lines, eliminating false sharing between the concurrent part_a and part_b threads of parallel_3 without requiring a mutex.

nodes.label: Lab5 Multithreading — Parallel-3 with Mutex + Cache-Line Padding
nodes.description: Combines both optimizations: cache-line-padded apple_t from lab5_parallel_3_cache_padding and mutex-protected add_and_assign from lab5_parallel_3_mutex, measuring the combined effect on throughput.

edges.label: lab4's patient() and doctor() functions are structurally identical to lab2/task1; a developer implements the mutex/semaphore/condition-variable hospital pattern in lab2/task1 first and directly reuses it for lab4.
config.hpp defines operations::add/sub/mul/div called by serial.cpp; serial cannot compile without these bitwise arithmetic implementations.
serial.cpp establishes the apple/orange dual-problem structure, part_a/part_b signatures, and namespace layout; parallel_2 is the first step that introduces concurrent apple/orange thread execution.
parallel_2_affinity is a direct extension of parallel_2 that adds set_cpu() CPU pinning to the two concurrent solve functions.
parallel_3 extends parallel_2 by also parallelising apple_solution internally (part_a and part_b run concurrently), building on the apple/orange concurrent structure from parallel_2.
parallel_3_affinity adds per-thread CPU set bindings to each of the three concurrent threads introduced in parallel_3.
parallel_3_mutex adds mutex guards on the add_and_assign calls that race in parallel_3's concurrent part_a/part_b; the race only exists once parallel_3 is understood.
parallel_3_cache_padding pads apple_t to eliminate the false sharing between part_a and part_b that only arises in parallel_3's concurrent thread structure.
parallel_3_mutex_cache_padding adds cache-line padding on top of the mutex-protected variant, combining both synchronisation strategies.
parallel_3_mutex_cache_padding adds mutex synchronisation on top of the cache-padded variant, combining both optimizations (diamond pattern with lab5_parallel_3_mutex).
